comments:
Summary:
This paper proposes Context-Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CC-GANs), an application of conditional GANs to image in-painting where the generator fills a missing patch conditioned on surrounding pixels and the discriminator must distinguish completed images (real vs. in-painted). The discriminator’s learned features are then used in a semi-supervised classification setting by combining an adversarial objective with a supervised classification loss. A variant (CC-GAN2) also uses the generator’s full output as an additional negative. Experiments on STL-10 and PASCAL VOC 2007 show improvements over several prior unsupervised/semi-supervised methods; qualitative in-painting results are provided.

Strengths:
- The idea of using in-painting with adversarial loss to produce realistic negative samples for discriminator feature learning is intuitive and well-motivated: the task is closely related to object recognition and can leverage large unlabeled datasets.
- The CC-GAN formulation and the CC-GAN2 extension are straightforward, easy to implement, and integrate naturally with a supervised loss for semi-supervised training.
- Empirical results demonstrate competitive or improved performance on challenging benchmarks (STL-10, PASCAL) and the generator produces qualitatively plausible in-paintings.
- Use of a strong discriminator architecture (VGG-A’) and adherence to good GAN training practices (DCGAN guidelines, Adam settings) are sensible choices.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Comparisons: although the paper argues gains are due to the CC-GAN objective rather than the discriminator architecture, most baselines use different architectures (e.g. AlexNet). While a supervised VGG-A’ baseline is presented, more controlled comparisons (same architecture, same training budget) against close methods (e.g. Pathak et al.) would strengthen the claims.
- Ablations are limited. Key design choices — mask size and sampling strategy, value of λc, effect of CC-GAN vs CC-GAN2, impact of low-resolution conditioning, and generator capacity — are not explored in sufficient depth. These affect both representation learning and generator quality.
- Training details are somewhat sparse: specific architectures (layer counts, filter sizes, number of channels) are referenced but not fully enumerated in the main text, and it is not clear how sensitive results are to training hyperparameters. Details about stability and any tricks to prevent collapse would be helpful.
- Statistical reporting is incomplete in places: while STL-10 reports mean and std across folds, PASCAL numbers appear to be single-run mAPs; significance or variability is not given. The extremely large number of epochs on PASCAL (~5000) raises questions about overfitting and practicality.
- It would be valuable to see failure cases and more systematic qualitative analysis (e.g. when in-painting fails, how that correlates with discriminator feature quality).

Soundness:
The formulations of GAN, conditional GAN, the CC-GAN and CC-GAN2 objectives are mathematically consistent and sensible. The semi-supervised objective is a natural combination of adversarial and classification losses. The overall experimental methodology is reasonable, but some missing experimental controls and ablations leave open whether all observed gains are attributable to the proposed modeling choice versus other factors (architecture, data augmentation, training schedule).

Originality / Impact:
The idea of using context-based in-painting in a GAN framework for semi-supervised feature learning is a meaningful extension of prior work on conditional GANs and context encoders. The approach bridges ideas from unsupervised context prediction and adversarial learning and is likely to be of interest to the community. Originality is moderate — it builds on known components but applies them in a novel and effective combination for representation learning.

Suggestions for authors:
- Add controlled ablations isolating: (i) CC-GAN vs vanilla GAN semi-supervised training with identical discriminator architectures; (ii) CC-GAN vs CC-GAN2; (iii) effect of low-resolution conditional input; (iv) different mask sizes and sampling strategies.
- Provide full architectural and training details in an appendix (layer-wise specifications, parameter counts, learning schedules, any regularization). Include training curves to show convergence and stability.
- Report variability (std) for PASCAL mAPs or perform multiple runs to assess robustness.
- Include more qualitative failure cases and analyze how generator quality correlates with final classifier accuracy.
- Discuss limitations explicitly (scalability to higher-resolution images, dependence on generator capacity) and potential remedies.

Recommendation:
I recommend acceptance. This is a well-motivated, practically useful contribution that demonstrates competitive empirical performance and produces convincing qualitative results. With additional ablations and more detailed reporting the paper would be even stronger.
